description: Rules for the time between adventures: long-term projects, recovery, downtime activities, and the phase structure that frames them.

projects.description: Rules for long-term endeavors advanced during downtime.

focuses.long_term_project_clocks: Ambitious endeavors are tracked as segmented clocks advanced by downtime rolls, completing whenever the segments fill (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.research_downtime: Characters spend downtime researching questions, sites, or lore, converting time and resources into answers and advantages.
focuses.spell_transcription: Casters copy new spells into books or formula documents during downtime, paying time and materials per spell (as in D&D wizards' spellbooks).
focuses.base_improvements: Downtime is invested in upgrading the group's headquarters, unlocking facilities that grant ongoing benefits (as in crew upgrades in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.acquiring_assets: Characters spend downtime obtaining assets such as gear, vehicles, or services, with a roll determining the quality of what they secure (as in Blades in the Dark's acquire asset).
focuses.seasonal_activities: Downtime is measured in seasons, each committed wholesale to a single pursuit like study, labor, or preparation (as in Ars Magica or Pendragon).
focuses.delegated_projects: Projects can be handed to hirelings, contacts, or followers who advance them while the characters adventure, at the risk of delay or betrayal.

recovery.description: Rules for healing bodies and minds between adventures.

focuses.healing_downtime: Wounds and harm heal through dedicated downtime activity, so recovery competes against other downtime priorities (as in Blades in the Dark's recover action).
focuses.natural_healing_rates: Left to rest, characters recover a fixed amount per day or week, making serious injury a calendar problem (as in classic D&D's daily healing).
focuses.long_term_care: A caregiver providing sustained medical attention accelerates recovery, with medicine tests determining the improvement.
focuses.recovery_tests: Recovery is rolled rather than automatic: a test determines whether a condition clears, persists, or worsens (as in Mouse Guard or Torchbearer).
focuses.condition_recovery: Named conditions such as sick, afraid, or injured each specify their own downtime remedies, so recovery is handled per condition rather than as a single pool.
focuses.stress_relief_vice: Characters shed accumulated stress by indulging a personal vice or comfort during downtime (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.vice_overindulgence: Overindulging relief brings complications such as lost time, attracted trouble, or spilled secrets, making stress relief itself a gamble (as in Blades in the Dark).

activities.description: Rules for the discrete pursuits characters can undertake between adventures.

focuses.carousing_tables: Characters spend gold celebrating and roll on carousing tables that mix bonus XP with mishaps and morning-after complications (an OSR staple).
focuses.training_advancement: Skills or abilities improve through downtime training, often requiring teachers, facilities, fees, and tracked time.
focuses.crafting_downtime: Items are crafted between adventures at rates set by cost, tools, and checks, converting downtime into equipment.
focuses.business_management: Characters run businesses or holdings during downtime, rolling for profits, events, and growth.
focuses.gambling_games: Downtime gambling is resolved with its own mini-game or opposed rolls, risking wealth for windfalls and story hooks.
focuses.gathering_information: Characters work contacts and streets to gather information, with rolls setting the quality and detail of what they learn (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.retainer_recruitment: Downtime is spent finding and vetting hirelings and retainers, with offers, reputation, and loyalty rolls shaping who signs on.
focuses.family_events: A structured domestic phase resolves marriages, births, deaths, and family fortunes between adventuring years (as in Pendragon's winter phase).
focuses.worship_ceremonies: Characters attend or lead religious ceremonies during downtime to regain divine favor, magic, or blessings (as in RuneQuest's worship rites).
focuses.shopping_resupply: Restocking gear and consumables is a downtime step with availability and prices determined by settlement size or rolls.
focuses.patron_petitions: Characters petition patrons, guilds, or courts for backing, resolving favor and obligation through downtime social rolls.
focuses.day_job_rolls: A single roll representing weeks of ordinary work yields income between adventures (as in Pathfinder Society's day job checks).
focuses.festivals_holidays: Seasonal festivals and holy days grant one-off boons, contests, and social opportunities when downtime overlaps them.

structure.description: Rules that frame downtime as a phase: its length, budget, costs, and interruptions.

focuses.downtime_phases: Play formally alternates between adventure and downtime phases, each with its own rules and available actions (as in Blades in the Dark's free play, score, and downtime loop).
focuses.entanglements_complications: Each downtime the GM rolls entanglements, consequences of heat and faction standing that intrude on the group's plans (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.lifestyle_upkeep_costs: Characters pay recurring lifestyle costs matching their standard of living, with cheap living carrying social and recovery penalties (as in Shadowrun or Blades in the Dark).
focuses.downtime_action_budget: Each character receives a fixed number of downtime actions per interval, making activity selection a hard allocation choice (as in Blades in the Dark's two activities).
focuses.between_session_time: Campaign time advances between sessions, sometimes in strict one-to-one time, so absent characters live onward and deadlines march (as in West Marches play).
focuses.downtime_scenes: Downtime activities are played out as framed roleplay scenes rather than abstract picks when the table wants the spotlight there.
focuses.reducing_heat: Laying low or paying bribes during downtime reduces accumulated heat or wanted levels before they boil over (as in Blades in the Dark).
